items.prompt: Square matrices A and B (same order) are inverses of each other if and only if:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch03 Sec 3.7 Definition 6 / Exercise 3.4 Q1

items.prompt: Explain why a rectangular (non-square) matrix cannot have an inverse, and what condition B must satisfy to be the inverse of a square matrix A.

expected.rubric: For both products AB and BA to be defined and equal the identity, A and B must be square matrices of the same order; a rectangular matrix cannot satisfy this, so it has no inverse. B is the inverse of square A if AB = BA = I, in which case B = A-inverse (and the inverse, when it exists, is unique).
markPoints: Both AB and BA must be defined and equal I, forcing A and B to be square of the same order
A rectangular matrix cannot meet this, so has no inverse
B is the inverse of A iff AB = BA = I (and the inverse is unique)
